findings.title: The keyless/mock default undermines the credibility the product sells: MockProvider echoes the deterministic signal, emits zero discrepancies, and ships a templated roadmap on every default-path surface
findings.expected: The credible, LLM-scored output the product is built around (self-audit discrepancies, repo-specific roadmap, AI-nuanced score) is what a first-time Character sees on the path they actually land on.
findings.got: Under LLM_PROVIDER=auto with no key the scan degrades to MockProvider, which sets each dimension score = signalScore verbatim and hard-sets discrepancies:[] — so the 'Flagged for review' self-audit panel never renders, the score has zero LLM nuance, and the roadmap falls back to a static per-dimension CATALOG personalized only by the numeric score. This same mock path backs the public report (Sam, Tomas), the D2 testing read (Oliver), the merge-blocking gate verdict (Raj), and the README badge on cache miss (Mei). The honest mocks (Demo badge, degraded-scan not persisted, partial-coverage warnings) are present and correct, but the credibility-bearing layer is absent from the default path.
findings.l2_priority: Run ONE live claude-cli scan + gate on a repo the panel knows cold and compare to the mock path: confirm real re-traceable discrepancies (not []), a repo-specific roadmap that names a file/config/count, an AI-nuanced score that reconciles, and a gate provider line reading claude not mock. This single run resolves the dominant theme for Sam, Tomas, Oliver, Raj, Mei, Dana.
findings.suggested_acceptance: On the live provider the discrepancy/roadmap/score surfaces are populated and repo-specific; on the keyless path the credibility-bearing surfaces are explicitly labelled as requiring the LLM (not silently rendered as if AI-scored).

findings.title: On the default (mock/fallback) path the roadmap/remediation prose is a generic per-dimension catalog — only the score is repo-specific
findings.expected: Each recommendation names a repo-specific, evidence-grounded highest-leverage move ('pin these 3 unpinned Actions to SHAs; gate the advisory coverage check') — not a generic dimension restatement a senior would reject.
findings.got: The keyless/fallback roadmap is the static CATALOG ranked by weighted upside, with the dimension's numeric score interpolated into the rationale but the title a generic gap statement. Read identically across many repos it is the 'add agent guidance / add more tests' boilerplate Priya's staff engineers and Dana would reject; the leverage RANKING is evidence-true but the move TEXT is templated. The repo-specific artifact (onboarding SKILL.md) is DB-gated/undiscoverable. No mutation/assertion/flaky/contract testing move exists in the catalog (Oliver).
findings.l2_priority: Run a live claude-cli scan on 2-3 seeded repos and judge whether the generated roadmap reads repo-specific (names files/configs/counts and senior-grade testing moves) vs the generic catalog seen by default on the gate/dashboard.
findings.suggested_acceptance: On a known repo the top roadmap item references a concrete file/config/count from that repo's evidence; on the gate/dashboard path the repo's real detected evidence is shown next to each recommendation, or the path defaults to the live provider.

findings.title: The merge-blocking gate verdict is a deterministic signal threshold, not an AI judgement, on the default/mock path
findings.expected: A gate made a required, merge-blocking check evaluates real maturity and blocks for an evidence-backed reason defensible to a blocked developer.
findings.got: Both the public gate API (default mock=true) and the App-mode PR Check Run (mock:true by design, 'fast and free of LLM spend') score with MockProvider, so score = signalScore exactly; the Action defaults live:false. The blocking verdict is a threshold over file/PR/governance detectors — the LLM nuance the product sells is absent by default, and on a keyless deploy even --live falls back to mock. (Note: for a GATE, determinism is arguably correct — but the dashboard advertises an AI-nuanced score and nothing on the Check Run says which path scored a blocked PR.)
findings.l2_priority: Run /api/gate/<repo>?mock=0 with claude-cli; confirm the sticky-comment provider line is not mock and the live verdict agrees with the default mock verdict on pass/fail for the same repo. Divergence = the default gate blocks on a different bar than the dashboard.
findings.suggested_acceptance: The merge-blocking verdict in its recommended config is evidence-cited AND reflects the same scoring path the dashboard/report shows; OR the mock-vs-live distinction is surfaced on the Check Run.

findings.title: Governance read is token-gated and additive-only — no token means blank/under-read, and missing guardrails can never demote a repo's score
findings.expected: The governance panel reflects real ruleset/required-check state and flags a repo lacking required checks; an ungoverned repo reads ungoverned, not protected, and cannot quietly pass the gate.
findings.got: (a) Without a GITHUB_TOKEN the rulesets/branch read is unavailable, getOrgGovernance returns null, and the tab shows a 'need a GitHub token' empty state — blind exactly during the quarterly audit. (b) applyGovernanceSignals is additive-only: present guardrails BOOST D3/D6/D8 but their ABSENCE is explicitly neutral, so a genuinely unprotected repo is not penalized in score/posture and can clear the gate on score alone. The per-repo table DOES show truthful protected/checks/signed flags + an 'unprotected' chip, so the read isn't green-by-default; the SCORE just won't punish ungoverned repos.
findings.l2_priority: Seed an org WITH a GITHUB_TOKEN; confirm the governance table matches the real ruleset state of a known-ungoverned repo. Then confirm whether that unprotected repo can still PASS the gate purely on score (green-by-default relocated from table to gate).
findings.suggested_acceptance: A genuinely unprotected repo cannot clear the gate purely on score; the additive-only blend is documented at the gate, or the gate floors on missing protection.

findings.title: D2 (and D8) measure test PRESENCE and QUANTITY, never test QUALITY — a 200-snapshot assertion-free suite scores ~80/100
findings.expected: The D2 read reflects test quality (assertion density / behavioral coverage / mutation) so a large assertion-light or AI-inflated suite does NOT read as mature.
findings.got: The D2 detector scores entirely from path-regex file counts (base 20-50 by bucket), presence-only framework/e2e/coverage-config string matches, and a file-count test-to-source ratio; it never reads a test body for assertions. 'Coverage tracking configured' (+10) is presence of a config string, not a real %. Mutation/contract/perf/a11y/schema are presence-only string matches capped 6-8 pts. A suite that asserts nothing scores base 50 + framework 15 + ratio 15 = 80 (L4 band). The LLM can't save it: clamped to +/-25 of the presence signal, handed <=4 test files, and absent entirely on the keyless path.
findings.l2_priority: On a repo with a known assertion-light/snapshot-heavy suite, confirm the live D2 reads as mature (L3+) and neither the LLM summary nor the guardband corrects it down; then a repo where Copilot doubled the test count — does D2 rise on volume alone?
findings.suggested_acceptance: D2 incorporates at least a directional assertion-quality signal (assertion density sampled from fetched test bodies, or a parsed mutation score) so a high-count assertion-free suite cannot reach the same band as a behaviorally-tested one.

findings.title: /trends has only a single baseline point out of the box — he cannot PROVE the D2 number moved
findings.expected: /trends shows the D2 score over time so Oliver can point at the line and say 'the gating initiative moved it' — proof of improvement.
findings.got: The seed scans each repo exactly once at a single date; commit-SHA dedup means re-running adds no points. /trends renders 'Only a baseline scan so far' and the forecast is null until two distinct scan days. The per-dimension D2 small-multiple machinery works but has nothing to plot.
findings.l2_priority: Seed multiple dated scans with VARYING D2 values (distinct commits); confirm the D2 small-multiple draws a moving line + delta and a trend dot deep-links to the pinned report — the make-or-break for 'prove it moved.'
findings.suggested_acceptance: Out of the box (or via a documented seed) /trends can show a multi-point D2 line with movement and deep-links, so the 'prove the number moved' job is demonstrable.

findings.title: The README badge renders a deterministic MOCK level by default — it can disagree with the credible LLM report
findings.expected: The badge in her README reflects the same credible, LLM-scored level she saw on the report — a badge that contradicts the report is worse than no badge.
findings.got: On a cache miss the badge endpoint runs scanRepository(..., { mock: true }) — the deterministic rubric, not the LLM scan. The badge only matches the real report when an LLM scan was already cached for that exact head SHA. The generator footnote admits this, but a maintainer pasting the badge URL cold gets the mock level, and after a push the SHA-keyed cache misses again.
findings.l2_priority: Scan a repo (LLM), then load its badge — confirm badge level == report level for the same head; then push a commit and confirm whether the badge silently reverts to the mock level until a re-scan.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Badge resolves to the most recent LLM report for the repo (any commit, with a freshness note) rather than minting a divergent mock level, OR the generator makes 'scan first' a required step, not a footnote.

findings.title: The /badge generator is undiscoverable — no link from the header, footer, or the report
findings.expected: After reading her score, a clear 'Get the README badge' CTA on the report (the OpenSSF Scorecard flow: see results -> grab the badge).
findings.got: /badge exists and works, but nothing in the UI links to it. The report header offers Export PDF and Onboarding skill but no badge CTA; SiteHeader nav and SiteFooter omit it entirely.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm live there is no badge entry point reachable by click from / or the report (search rendered DOM, not just .tsx).
findings.suggested_acceptance: A 'Get README badge' link on the report header (and/or footer) deep-links to /badge with her repo prefilled.

findings.title: Policy drift: the Security (D9) floor enforced on the dashboard + App check is silently dropped from the copyable CI snippet / action.yml
findings.expected: Editing the gate policy once (incl. the 'Security floor D9>=50' checkbox) changes ALL THREE enforcement surfaces — dashboard, App PR check, and the CI snippet copied into pipelines. One source of truth, no drift.
findings.got: The dashboard fleet eval (evaluateGateLite) and the App PR check (evaluateGate) both honor minDimensionFor.D9, but ciWith()/gateQuery() omit minDimensionFor entirely and action.yml has no min-security/per-dimension input — so the copied snippet enforces a WEAKER bar than the dashboard. policyFromParams only reconstructs D9 via a separate ?security= param the generated snippet never sets. The Governance page literally claims 'no drift… enforce it everywhere.' A repo green in CI could be red on the dashboard.
findings.l2_priority: Set the Security floor in the editor, read the rendered CI snippet, confirm whether it includes a security/min-dimension arg; diff dashboard pass-rate vs what the gateQuery returns for a D9-failing repo.
findings.suggested_acceptance: When minDimensionFor.D9 is set, ciWith() emits a security:'true' (or min-security:'N') line AND action.yml forwards it to --min-security, so all three surfaces enforce the identical D9 floor.

findings.title: The .ai/ standard + onboarding SKILL.md generator is undiscoverable from her entry points (lives only on the per-repo report header)
findings.expected: From practices/plan/governance — where she authors and rolls out the standard — she can find and generate the .ai/ standard + SKILL.md.
findings.got: The generator is GET /api/report/skill, surfaced ONLY as the 'Onboarding skill' download on the per-repo ReportHeader. None of practices/plan/governance link to it; she'd reach it only by drilling into an individual repo's report via a Practice Library exemplar link.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm whether a platform lead starting on /practices can locate the .ai/ standard / SKILL.md generator without prior knowledge of the report route; time-to-discover is the metric.
findings.suggested_acceptance: A 'Generate the .ai/ standard / onboarding skill' affordance on /practices (per repo, or fleet-level), not only the individual report header.

findings.title: Contributors 'Involvement' table is a full per-named-engineer commit/AI-commit scoreboard with CSV export — the surveillance read despite the 'not a scoreboard' caption
findings.expected: No surface invites him to rank named individuals; per-person output is not presented in a way he'd be embarrassed to have a report see over his shoulder.
findings.got: The Involvement table renders up to 50 rows, each a named login with raw commit count, AI-commit count, AI-share bar, repos touched, last-active — ranked by commits desc. The 'context to explore, not a scoreboard' caption is text over a table that is structurally an individual-output ranking, and it is exportable as CSV — precisely the spreadsheet leadership weaponizes.
findings.l2_priority: Live: does the rendered involvement table read as a per-person leaderboard to an EM despite the caption? Test whether removing/aggregating raw per-person commit counts (or collapsing behind opt-in) preserves the 'who could seed a thin repo' value without the scoreboard feel; confirm whether the CSV export is defensible.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Any per-named-individual output is framed and ordered so it cannot be read as a performance ranking; raw per-person commit counts are not the primary sort key, or the view is opt-in.

findings.title: Small-population champion guard is inconsistent: the Adoption tab's 'AI champions' card and the Teams tab's per-team champion chips have NO contributor-count suppression
findings.expected: The same small-population vanity guard that protects the Contributors tab applies wherever named champions are celebrated.
findings.got: Adoption's 'AI champions' card maps a.champions with no >=3 (or per-team population) gate, so a 1-2 person org still celebrates a named '#1' culture carrier. Teams cards render named champion chips with no team-size guard — a 2-person CODEOWNERS team surfaces a named individual with their AI%. Contributors got this right; these two did not.
findings.l2_priority: Live: on a thinly-populated seeded team/org, confirm whether Adoption/Teams celebrate a named individual that Contributors correctly suppresses. The inconsistency itself is the finding.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Champion/culture-carrier UI on Adoption and Teams applies the same population-size threshold as Contributors (org-level >=3 contributors; team-level a minimum team size) before naming an individual.

findings.title: The 'one move' is offered as a ranked exploration list, not a decision — and the Briefing outsources the actual recommended actions to an external LLM
findings.expected: The overview names ONE (or two) highest-leverage fleet moves tied to a dimension/team and the cited evidence — a decision Dana can carry to the board.
findings.got: OrgLeverageMoves ranks up to 5 systemic gaps by a real leverage formula but is titled 'Gaps to explore across the fleet' and captioned 'inputs to explore… not a to-do list'; its CTA is 'Browse all repositories.' The executive Briefing's recommended-actions step is a 'Copy briefing for LLM' payload whose Ask defers '3 highest-leverage actions' to whatever LLM Dana pastes into — the product won't state the move itself.
findings.l2_priority: On a live claude-cli-seeded /org, judge whether the #1 leverage move and the LLM-generated Briefing actions read as a single defensible decision a VP puts on a slide — or a hedged exploration list she still has to synthesize. The ranking math is present and sound; the gap is framing/decisiveness.
findings.suggested_acceptance: The overview (or Briefing) surfaces a single named #1 move with its projected fleet-maturity gain and the specific dimension/repos it lifts, on-screen, without a copy-to-LLM round trip.

findings.title: Bedrock no-training / in-boundary guarantee is invisible at the point of private-scan decision (buried in README/docs)
findings.expected: Before scanning private code, from the product itself, she can confirm a Bedrock / in-boundary, no-training, not-shared-with-providers path exists — legible at the decision point, not buried in docs.
findings.got: The /connect surface only promises Ascent 'stores only the derived scores and evidence, never your source' — a GitHub-source claim. The LLM no-training/in-boundary guarantee is rendered nowhere a user sees it: it lives in README.md:151-152 and code comments. The only UI mention of 'Bedrock' is the Enterprise (Custom) pricing card with no no-training wording. Default LLM_PROVIDER=auto routes to Gemini-or-mock, not Bedrock ('Never silently selects Bedrock'), and no UI states which provider processed the code.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm live whether ANY in-product UI discloses the private-code routing + the Bedrock no-training/in-boundary guarantee before/at a private scan. If still docs-only, the major stands.
findings.suggested_acceptance: On /connect (and the onboarding App path), before a private scan runs, the product states which provider processes the code and that the Bedrock path is in-boundary / no-training / not-shared-with-providers — in rendered UI, not only docs.

findings.title: No numeric pricing on any public surface — private-scan rate is 'Prepaid credits / final rate TBD', Enterprise is 'Custom / Contact us'
findings.expected: Actual numbers reachable from the landing page — what a private scan costs, where the paid tier starts — with no contact-sales wall (his #1 scored criterion).
findings.got: Both /#pricing and /pricing show tiers + feature lists but zero dollar figures, by explicit design. Private = 'Prepaid credits' note 'Indicative; final rate TBD'; Enterprise CTA = 'Contact us'. 'TBD' and 'talk to us' is his shortlist-drop trigger.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm live that no pricing page or checkout step ever surfaces a per-credit/per-seat number before sign-up; confirm the Enterprise path is the only 'contact' route.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Show at least one real number on a public surface — e.g. '$X per private scan credit' or 'Pro from $Y/mo' — so the freemium ladder isn't 'Free -> talk to us'.

findings.title: 'Onboarding skill' and 'Export PDF' links render on every public report but 503 without a DB
findings.expected: If an affordance is offered, it works on this Character's reachable surface — or it isn't shown.
findings.got: ReportHeader renders both links unconditionally. On the keyless/DB-off public path /api/report/skill returns 503 'Skill export requires a database'. Sam clicks the one artifact he'd judge for repo-specificity and gets an error — the worst moment to look unserious to a skeptic.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm whether the UAT env (PGlite on) makes these links work; if so this is keyless-only. Either way: gate the link on report persistence, or downgrade gracefully, rather than 503.
findings.suggested_acceptance: The skill/PDF affordance is hidden (or shows an inline 'needs history' hint) when no persisted report exists, never a raw 503.

findings.title: Drill is fleet -> repo, not fleet -> team; Dana's 'why is the platform team yellow?' question routes through a repo leaderboard she must re-aggregate mentally
findings.expected: Drill fleet -> TEAM -> dimension -> cited repo evidence, with levels/posture agreeing at the team layer (she thinks in ~18 teams / 4 product lines, not repos).
findings.got: The headline drill goes to /repositories (a per-repo leaderboard) and on to /report?repo=… A /teams tab exists, but the overview's leverage moves, gaps, and movers are repo-keyed, so the team layer isn't the spine of the drill — she'd partly re-average teams herself.
findings.l2_priority: Open /org/<slug>/teams and /segments live: does the team/segment view give per-team maturity + posture + adoption/rigor that reconciles with the fleet number and drills to its repos' evidence? If yes, navigation nit; if thin, a real reconciliation gap for an 18-team org.

findings.title: On the default mock path the gate uses, the remediation prose is a generic per-dimension template — only the score is repo-specific
findings.expected: Every 'you're failing' comes with repo-specific context + fix; a senior wouldn't read the same 'add agent guidance' line across many repos.
findings.got: Scores + provenance are well-grounded, but the dashboard/App-check scan with mock:true, and on the mock/fallback path the roadmap comes from a static 9-template CATALOG personalized only by injecting the dimension's numeric score. The 'cheapest path to green' links to the right practice, but the recommendation prose itself reads as a template until a live LLM scan runs.
findings.l2_priority: Run a live claude-cli scan on 2-3 seeded repos; judge whether the roadmap reads repo-specific vs the generic catalog seen by default on the gate/dashboard.
findings.suggested_acceptance: On the gate/dashboard path, surface the repo's real detected evidence next to each recommendation (not just the score), or default the org dashboard to the live provider.

findings.title: Corpus benchmark / percentile is null below 5 repos — a small seeded org shows '—' where Dana expects a 'vs peers' number
findings.expected: A 'where do we stand vs others' read she can cite, or an honest absence — not a confidently-wrong percentile.
findings.got: getOrgBenchmark suppresses the headline percentile below CORPUS_MIN=5 and the peer cohort below COHORT_MIN=5 (returns null), rendering '—'/'no corpus yet'. Statistically the RIGHT call, but on a freshly-seeded single-org local corpus Dana sees no peer comparison.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm whether the seeded corpus has >=5 other-org repos so a percentile renders; if not, the 'vs peers' criterion is unevaluable live and the absence is a seed limitation.

findings.title: Single-repo grounding samples at most 32 files / 180KB — thin for a real team service (a monorepo or large backend)
findings.expected: The score on a main service is grounded in enough of the real repo that the Character would stake a VP conversation on it.
findings.got: Ingestion caps at MAX_FILES=32, MAX_FILE_BYTES=14k, MAX_TOTAL_BYTES=180k; the prompt window caps file excerpts further. The picker is signal-aware (curated high-signal files), and deterministic detectors read fuller content, so the signal floor mitigates; but for a large service the LLM nuance sees a thin slice.
findings.l2_priority: Scan a larger team service/monorepo and judge whether the cited evidence is representative enough to defend the number, or whether the 32-file cap produces a visibly shallow read on a big repo.

findings.title: Anonymous public scan has NO PR/branch-protection evidence — D3/D6/D7/D8 are file/commit-only
findings.expected: The read includes 'is the flaky build gated?', 'are reviews required?', 'is AI in PRs governed?' — PR + branch-protection facts.
findings.got: prStats/governance are token-gated; a keyless public scan gets null for both, so the rigor-axis dimensions reflect only repo-tree + commit signals. The report DOES warn honestly ('PR signals were skipped — they need a GitHub token'), but it caps how far the read reconciles with the actual review/gate discipline — and risks reading a well-governed repo as ungoverned.
findings.l2_priority: With a GITHUB_TOKEN set, confirm D3/D6/D8 evidence populates (review coverage %, status-checks-required, AI-governed rate) and reconciles with the repo's real branch protection; confirm the 'Heads up' warning renders prominently so the buyer isn't misled.

findings.title: Engine guardband can cap an LLM that's correctly contradicting a wrong detector
findings.expected: When the detector is demonstrably wrong, the truth should win — the score should match the repo.
findings.got: The LLM score is clamped to +/-25 of the signal BEFORE the blend. If a detector badly under/over-scores a dimension, the guardband structurally prevents the LLM from fully correcting it — the discrepancy is surfaced (good) but the NUMBER stays anchored to the wrong signal. A defensible anti-hallucination design, but a skeptic who knows the detector missed will see the score not move enough.
findings.l2_priority: Pick a repo whose test/CI layout the detector mis-reads; confirm the discrepancy is flagged AND judge whether the +/-25-anchored blended score still misleads. If so, the discrepancy panel must be prominent enough to trust the caveat over the number.

findings.title: No quantified third-party proof — marketing is self-claim narrative; the example ROI numbers are hypotheticals, not customer outcomes
findings.expected: A specific, quantified customer result OR the live scan standing as proof.
findings.got: /about is qualitative with one illustrative hypothetical ('moves 6 of 8 repos to L3 by Q3'); no case study, no quantified customer result, no logo wall. So the live scan output is the only proof he can lean on.
findings.l2_priority: Decide whether the live scan output alone is strong enough to carry 'proof'; if the scan reads mock/thin, this minor becomes a major.

findings.title: 'Pricing' nav points to the on-page anchor, not the dedicated /pricing page; /pricing is effectively undiscoverable from nav
findings.expected: A consistent path to the fuller pricing/tier comparison.
findings.got: Header and footer both link '/#pricing' (the landing anchor). The richer /pricing page (4-tier comparison incl. Pro/Team seats + retention) is reachable only by URL or a quota-upgrade CTA — a buyer never lands on it.

findings.title: /about leads with org-installation CTAs ('Scan your org' -> /connect, 'Explore demo' -> /org/vercel), not the free public scan
findings.expected: If a buyer reaches /about first, the frictionless free single-repo scan should be the obvious next step.
findings.got: Both /about CTAs route to /connect (org install -> sign-in) and /org/vercel (needs a populated DB). The free public ScanForm is only on '/'. A buyer who opens /about cold sees a heavier front door.

findings.title: Whole-org read via /onboarding is a disclosed MOCK preview on the public-handle path — not live scores
findings.expected: A fast first whole-org read that reconciles with her sense of her teams.
findings.got: Without a GitHub App installation + org credits, the onboarding import runs a deterministic MOCK preview (mock:true default), explicitly disclosed; real LLM scores require the App path + credits. So her org-wide scores are preview-grade unless she installs the App on her own private org.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm the preview is clearly labeled in the done-state and dashboard so a CTO never mistakes preview scores for a live fleet read; verify the App-path live org scan produces reconciling per-squad scores.

findings.title: Regression alerts and the weekly digest have no in-app surface — only an audit row + a webhook POST
findings.expected: I can see what would have paged me, and the alert leaves an audit trail I can review before wiring it into Slack.
findings.got: The audit trail exists ('scan.regression' recorded even with no sink) and the Slack payload explains WHY, but there's no dashboard view of recent alerts/digests; to evaluate whether it cries wolf, Raj must configure a webhook sink and watch it fire from cron.
findings.l2_priority: Wire a test webhook (or capture via the audit tab), trigger a real demotion via two seeded scans, confirm exactly one alert fires with the right WHY attributions, and a re-scan with no further drop does NOT re-page.

findings.title: 'Requires review' rate counts requires-a-PR, not requires-an-approval — an enforcement gradation she'd want made explicit
findings.expected: Per SOC 2 CC8.1, 'requires review' means an approval is actually required before merge (required_approving_review_count >= 1) — not merely that a PR is opened.
findings.got: getOrgGovernance.requireReviewRate counts g.requiresPullRequest (= a pull_request rule exists), NOT requiredApprovals >= 1. fetchBranchGovernance DOES capture requiredApprovals and requiresCodeOwnerReview, but the fleet rate and the 'Requires review' UI row use the weaker predicate. A repo with a PR rule but 0 required approvals counts as 'requires review'.
findings.l2_priority: On the seeded fleet, check whether the 'Requires review' % visibly overstates approval-enforced coverage (repos with a PR rule but 0 required approvals counted as compliant).
findings.suggested_acceptance: The 'Requires review' coverage reflects required_approving_review_count >= 1 (and ideally surfaces CODEOWNERS-required-review).

findings.title: Supply-chain card hides entirely when SUPPLY_CHAIN_PROVIDER=off — no 'supply-chain scanning not enabled' affordance
findings.expected: When advisory scanning is off she should still see the surface exists and how to turn it on, rather than the section silently not rendering.
findings.got: The card is wrapped in {supply && supply.scanned > 0 && …} (and getOrgSupplyChain returns null when provider=off), so with the default 'off' provider there is NO supply-chain card and no hint it could be enabled. Under the mock seed this is moot, but the absence is silent.
findings.l2_priority: Lower priority — under the mock seed the card is present and labelled. Consider an 'enable supply-chain scanning' empty-state when provider=off.
findings.suggested_acceptance: When provider=off, show a one-line 'Supply-chain advisory scanning not enabled (set SUPPLY_CHAIN_PROVIDER)' so absence is explained, not silent.

findings.title: Org tabs + audit export need ASCENT_OPEN_ORG_DASHBOARDS in addition to ASCENT_AUTH_BYPASS; bypass alone degrades to a 'No access' empty state
findings.expected: Per the journey seed (ASCENT_AUTH_BYPASS=1 + seeded org), the security/governance/audit surfaces open.
findings.got: canReadOrg and /api/audit's requireOrgRead both resolve to openOrgDashboardsEnabled() when OAuth is unconfigured. authBypassEnabled flips authGateEnabled/getViewer but NOT canReadOrg's auth-off branch, so org reads require the SEPARATE flag ASCENT_OPEN_ORG_DASHBOARDS=1. The seed workflow sets it, but the journey seed line names only the bypass.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm the live run env carries BOTH ASCENT_AUTH_BYPASS=1 and ASCENT_OPEN_ORG_DASHBOARDS=1; if only the bypass is set, every org tab renders 'No access' and the journey can't start.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Documented two-flag dependency is acceptable; ensure the seed/UAT env sets both.

findings.title: Supply-chain card and CODEOWNERS-required-review have no dedicated export path; only the LLM brief + per-row audit CSV exist
findings.expected: For the evidence binder she wants the Dependabot advisory totals and the enforcement gradations exportable, not just pasteable into an LLM.
findings.got: Security tab offers a PDF + 'Copy security brief for LLM'; Governance offers 'Copy governance brief for LLM' only. Only the AUDIT trail has a true filtered CSV. The advisory counts and requiresCodeOwnerReview don't reach a structured export.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm the Security PDF renders the supply-chain section and is binder-grade; decide whether a structured (CSV) security/governance export is wanted beyond the PDF + LLM brief.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Out of scope at L1 — the PDF + audit CSV cover the attestation need; a structured posture CSV is a nice-to-have.

findings.title: Deterministic D2 roadmap item is the generic 'few tests vouch for behavior', not specific senior-QA moves (mutation/assertion signal, flaky quarantine, contract tests)
findings.expected: The roadmap names specific, correctly-prioritized testing moves a senior QA lead would endorse — gating before more tests, a quality signal before chasing coverage, flaky quarantine, contract tests at the seam.
findings.got: The fallback/mock D2 catalog entry is a single generic gap. Gating is named — but as a D3 item, not a D2 quality move; no mutation/assertion-density, flaky-quarantine, or contract-test recommendation exists in the catalog. Weighted-upside ranking can surface gating ahead of volume (correct directionally).
findings.l2_priority: With an LLM key, judge whether the GENERATED D2/D8 roadmap names senior-grade moves (mutation, assertion density, flaky quarantine, contract tests at seams) or collapses to 'add more tests / raise coverage'.

findings.title: Recommendation status changes 403 for public-org scans — Oliver's default public scan can't move a rec open->in-progress->done
findings.expected: He can move a testing recommendation open->in-progress->done and see it reflected.
findings.got: PATCH /api/recommendations/[id] returns 403 when the rec's owning org is the shared 'public' funnel org — and a keyless public scan persists under orgSlug 'public'. ASCENT_AUTH_BYPASS=1 opens the authz check but does NOT lift the public-org 403. The tracker is fully functional only for recs in a real (non-public) org. The tracker UI itself (optimistic update, rollback) is solid.
findings.l2_priority: Under bypass+PGlite, import a repo into a NAMED org and confirm a D2 rec moves open->in_progress->done and persists; confirm the public-org path shows a sensible message rather than a dead 403.

findings.title: The generated manifest ships core boundary fields (neverTouch, secretsFrom, agents, blank-description purpose) as TODOs
findings.expected: A senior platform lead's .ai/ standard adapted to the repo — not boilerplate with TODOs left as the deliverable.
findings.got: Commands, languages, archetype, typecheck, generatedFrom, and (when present) purpose are repo-derived and the doctor proves them. But neverTouch/secretsFrom/agents and per-module CONTEXT are intentional TODOs the RUNNING agent fills against live code (the design says a scan can't know them, and the guardrails forbid fabricating them). Honest, and the doctor flags leftover TODOs — but at generation time the artifact is a strong scaffold + TODOs.
findings.l2_priority: Generate the SKILL.md/standard for a seeded repo and judge: is the TODO surface small enough that a senior would call it 'adapted to this repo', or a template she'd reject? Assess the generated artifact (pre-run).

findings.title: Governance copy promises 'no drift' explicitly — true for dashboard/App-check/CI on the basic bars, but overclaims given the D9 gap
findings.expected: Copy that matches behavior.
findings.got: 'The dashboard gate and your pipeline run the identical policy — no drift' and 'change it once, enforce it everywhere'. True for minLevel/minOverall/minDimension/posture; false for the Security floor (see PRIYA-01). Priya checks for drift and will catch the mismatch.

findings.title: Per-dimension evidence is descriptive text, not clickable file links
findings.expected: Like the OpenSSF Scorecard, each dimension's evidence links into the repo at the file it's about, so she can verify the claim.
findings.got: Evidence is concrete and repo-specific but rendered as plain text. There is no hyperlink to the file/line that produced it. The provenance track shows signal->LLM->blended numerically, which partly satisfies 'show your work', but she cannot click through to the underlying file.
findings.l2_priority: Open a dimension card and confirm the evidence items are not links; judge whether descriptive evidence + provenance track is enough to vouch publicly without click-through.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Evidence items that name a file link to that path on github.com at the scanned ref.

findings.title: The PR maturity gate Action requires a self-hosted Ascent deployment (ascent-url)
findings.expected: Install a published Action once, like Scorecard's action — no infrastructure to run.
findings.got: action.yml requires ascent-url (the URL of an Ascent deployment serving /api/gate). A solo unpaid maintainer has nowhere to point it unless the project hosts a public gate endpoint. The scan/report/badge still fully deliver without it.
findings.l2_priority: Confirm whether the hosted Ascent deployment exposes a public /api/gate Mei could point ascent-url at without self-hosting.
findings.suggested_acceptance: Either a hosted public gate endpoint documented for OSS use, or action.yml docs clearly state self-hosting is required.

findings.title: STRENGTH: the score reconciles and is glass-box — per-dimension signal/LLM/blended provenance with a +/-25 guardband, a waterfall that sums to the headline, and loud partial-coverage warnings
findings.expected: Numbers that add up and that a Character can drill to evidence to defend out loud.
findings.got: Each dimension exposes signalScore, llmScore, and the blended score with the +/-LLM_GUARDBAND visualized in a ProvenanceTrack SVG; the LLM can nuance but cannot contradict the deterministic signal; overall is a renormalized weighted mean decomposed by a waterfall that sums to the headline; the engine emits explicit warnings when the LLM didn't validate all dims. Reconciliation is structurally enforced. The single thing most likely to make a skeptic say 'that's actually right.'

findings.title: STRENGTH: detectors grade substance, not file presence (the anti-'CONTRIBUTING.md +1' design)
findings.expected: Not 'has AGENTS.md? +1' — judge whether it's real and followed.
findings.got: D1 grades guidance CONTENT (commands, architecture, constraints, advanced tooling); D9 distinguishes present vs. CI-wired; the .ai/ standard is scored by evidence-of-use (Goodhart guard — empty scaffold barely scores, memory must have >=2 entries).

findings.title: STRENGTH: D8 rewards an AI EVAL/HARNESS, not mere AI usage — and keeps 'is AI used' as a separate, unscored indicator
findings.expected: D8 must reward a real AI eval/test harness, so 'we use Copilot' can't masquerade as 'we test our AI'.
findings.got: D8's top signal (+30) is an AI-output eval/golden-test harness (evals/, promptfoo, golden/); 'is AI in the workflow' is computed SEPARATELY as a non-scored indicator (detectAiUsage), explicitly so AI usage isn't conflated with maturity. The .ai/ doctor is scored by evidence-of-use. Presence-not-execution (see D2 majors), but the design intent is right.

findings.title: STRENGTH: Dependabot advisory signal is architecturally separated from D9 and labelled demo under mock — the central trust gate holds
findings.expected: Advisory counts shown alongside the deterministic D9 rubric, never folded into the score; mock data flagged as demo so she'd never attest to fabricated counts.
findings.got: getOrgSupplyChain is consumed ONLY by the Security tab + its LLM brief, never by the scoring engine/prompt/analyzer; D9 is a file-presence detector. The supply-chain card literally says 'Demo data — set SUPPLY_CHAIN_PROVIDER=github for live alerts' when provider=mock, and the brief tags the section '(Dependabot — demo data)'. The single most defensible design in the sweep.

findings.title: STRENGTH: the gate is deterministic, fail-closed, evidence-cited, archetype-aware — and the dashboard + App check run the IDENTICAL policy
findings.expected: A block I can explain: which policy, which dimension, which evidence — and a verdict that doesn't flip-flop on identical code.
findings.got: evaluateGate fail-closes on unscored dimensions (a missing Security/Testing dim FAILS rather than slips through), lists every failing condition with a specific message, and the sticky comment carries a per-dimension score->floor table + the active policy + the provider. The gate API reads/writes the SAME cache key as the requested mode (the old stochastic-flip bug is explicitly fixed). evaluateGate (App check) and evaluateGateLite (fleet) share identical rules over ONE persisted policy; defaults are archetype-aware (org L3/>=40/no-ungoverned vs solo L2/>=25). On a hard error the PR check posts a 'neutral' check with a Re-run button. A D2 floor is enforceable via min-dimension or minDimensionFor.

findings.title: STRENGTH: Regression alert is delta-only with per-tenant routing, audit trail, and exactly-once credit alerts — engineered against alert fatigue
findings.expected: Fire on real demotions only, never re-spam a known-bad repo, leave an audit trail, never page the wrong tenant.
findings.got: detectRegression fires only on a level demotion / slide-into-ungoverned / overall drop>=5 / dim drop>=15 — a small dip or a steady-bad repo does NOT fire. Audit recorded even with no sink; per-tenant webhook routing is pinned so org A never POSTs to org B; the whole path is throw-safe. Low-credits alerts fire exactly once on the threshold-crossing edge. The cron rescan is fail-closed on a missing CRON_SECRET, claims-before-work, captures prev BEFORE persisting (a real baseline), and uses the deployment's real provider.

findings.title: STRENGTH: trajectory/ETA shows its basis — OLS slope, R2 'trend confidence', latest-value anchor, 365-day sanity cap
findings.expected: An ETA with a defensible basis, not 'you'll reach L4 in Q3' from nowhere.
findings.got: Trajectory renders Now->projected, weekly rate, ETA(level, days, date), and R2 as 'trend confidence N% · noisy' (<50%). The fit is a real OLS over the per-day fleet trend, anchored on the latest actual value, capped at a year. Exactly the provenance a board-skeptic VP needs to defend a forecast.

findings.title: STRENGTH: adoption is honestly separated from rigor, onboarding can't fake fleet movement, and team attribution is a CODEOWNERS team not a person
findings.expected: 'Everyone uses Copilot' must not masquerade as 'we're AI-native'; period movement must be real; team reads attributable without naming individuals.
findings.got: Adoption (D1/D4/D7) and Rigor are distinct axis roll-ups shown as separate tiles/deltas and as the posture quadrant; period deltas are cohort-matched (only repos on both sides of the window) with onboarding reported separately as growth; the 'most AI-attributed team' resolves to a CODEOWNERS team name, not an individual.

findings.title: STRENGTH: Contributors tab suppresses the '#1 champion' celebration below 3 contributors, and surfaces bus-factor as risk-to-explore, never a directive at a person
findings.expected: Champion celebration suppressed when the population is too small to mean anything; key-person risk framed as cross-training input, never naming-and-shaming.
findings.got: The AI-champions block renders only when champions.length > 0 && totalContributors >= 3, with an inline comment naming the exact failure mode. The per-repo concentration table flags bus-factor <=1 and solo-maintainer repos as 'key-person risk' framed 'inputs to explore, never directives.' (The guard is missing on Adoption/Teams — see MARCUS-04.)

findings.title: STRENGTH: the path-to-green is earned, not mandated — closest-to-green worklist + per-gap deep-linked practice + one-click/batch starter PRs, framed as friction not a stick
findings.expected: A cheapest-path-to-green and a starter PR per gap, never a bare red list.
findings.got: Governance shows 'Cheapest path to green', each failing dimension deep-links to its practice, and PracticeApply opens a leak-free draft PR per gap with a fleet-wide batch rollout. Roadmap gaps are phrased as invitational 'explore' prompts, never directives.

findings.title: STRENGTH: the .ai/ standard's control placement is correct-by-construction and the doctor PROVES claims (pre-push primary, CI thin backstop, fail on unwired controls)
findings.expected: Controls placed correctly — pre-push primary, CI thin backstop — not boilerplate; a doctor that proves, not asserts.
findings.got: The manifest, skill body, and tracks matrix all encode shift-left; the doctor runs capability commands (--run), checks each pre-push control is actually wired into the repo's REAL hook with command-alias matching, and FAILs when pre-push controls have no local hook. Guardrails forbid fabricating commands/architecture.

findings.title: STRENGTH: Governance reads ENFORCEMENT (active rulesets) and names the gaps, not mere CODEOWNERS/branch-protection existence
findings.expected: Posture must reflect what's required across all repos, with the falling-short repos named.
findings.got: fetchBranchGovernance reads the rulesets API (/rules/branches) for the ACTIVE pull_request / required_status_checks / required_signatures rules + the protected flag — enforcement state, not file presence. The Security + Governance tabs name the unprotected repos and each failing repo's exact missed condition. CODEOWNERS is parsed for team attribution, deliberately not dressed up as a required-review control.

findings.title: STRENGTH: Audit CSV export is binder-grade — filtered, full-trail cursor-loop, attributable, timestamped, and formula-injection-hardened
findings.expected: An auditor's 'show me the change history' answered by an exportable, attributable, filterable trail — not a screenshot.
findings.got: /api/audit?format=csv cursor-loops ALL matching rows (10k cap) for the CURRENT filter set into RFC-4180 CSV with at/action/actorId/repo/level/overall/headSha/meta columns, dated attachment filename, and =+-@ formula-injection neutralization. Deterministic (at desc,id desc) keyset pagination. Org-scoped, no cross-tenant leak.

findings.title: STRENGTH: the no-signup/no-paywall public front door is genuinely honored in code, with streamed progress
findings.expected: Scan a public repo and read the full report with no account, no email, no payment; streaming so it never reads as hung.
findings.got: The public scan path never consults the login wall (gate only fires for non-public orgSlug). The weekly quota is a soft, fail-open nudge (default 3/week anon; no limit on a DB-less deploy) that salvages the last persisted report instead of walling out. ScanForm streams SSE stages with a sticky provider/region/fallback bar and typed timeout messages. Signing in only RAISES the limit.

findings.title: STRENGTH: honest accounting — warnings on low coverage / mock degrade / partial LLM coverage, and mock is badged 'Demo · deterministic rubric'
findings.expected: No latency theater, no success theater; tell me when the AI didn't really contribute.
findings.got: Mock/degrade is badged in the header ('Demo · deterministic rubric') and warned ('not fully AI-validated'); low coverage and truncation warn; SSE streams real per-stage progress; a degraded scan is not cached/persisted as canonical.

findings.title: STRENGTH: the badge generator emits clean paste-ready Markdown/HTML/AsciiDoc in level AND gate modes, linking back to the report
findings.expected: A single Shields-style badge with paste-ready snippets, level + pass/fail gate modes, that links back to evidence.
findings.got: BadgeGenerator offers level/score/gate kinds x flat/flat-square/for-the-badge styles, a live preview, a copy button, and all three formats — each snippet wraps the badge in a link to /report. The SVG endpoint adds CVD-safe level glyphs, a click-through href, and never discloses a private repo's level.
